skill.name: tdd-workflow
skill.description: Guides AI coding agents through test-driven development using the RED, GREEN, and REFACTOR cycle.
skill.summary: Apply a disciplined TDD workflow for tests, implementation, and refactoring.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: AI review found no evidence of weak cryptography, reconnaissance, prompt injection, or shell execution in SKILL.md. The static hits at lines 3, 15, 31, 43, and 48 are false positives from front matter text, a Markdown diagram fence, and TDD prose. The only residual risk is broad Bash tool permission at line 4, with no command instructions, so the skill is low risk and safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Broad Command Tool Permission
low_findings.description: The skill declares Bash in allowed-tools, which can enable external command execution in compatible agents. The body does not request shell execution, network access, or credential handling, so this is a low residual permission concern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash permission is directly present in the skill front matter. Confidence is below very high because the skill content gives no commands or malicious execution path.

content.value_statement: Developers often skip tests or write them after implementation. This skill keeps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused on RED, GREEN, and REFACTOR steps.
actual_capabilities: Explains the RED, GREEN, and REFACTOR cycle for coding tasks.
Promotes writing failing tests before production code.
Guides test structure with the Arrange, Act, Assert pattern.
Helps prioritize happy path, error case, edge case, and performance tests.
Identifies common TDD anti-patterns to avoid.
Suggests a multi-agent split for tests, implementation, and refactoring.
limitations: It provides workflow guidance, not a test runner or framework adapter.
It does not include language-specific test examples.
It cannot verify coverage unless the agent runs project tests separately.
It is less useful for exploratory spikes and visual layout work.

use_cases.title: Add a Feature with Tests First
use_cases.description: Use the skill to define expected behavior, write a failing test, implement only enough code, and then refactor.

use_cases.title: Fix a Regression Safely
use_cases.description: Capture the bug as a failing test before changing the implementation, then keep the fix small and focused.

use_cases.title: Coordinate AI Coding Agents
use_cases.description: Assign separate agents to write tests, implement passing code, and improve structure while preserving green tests.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple TDD Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the tdd-workflow skill for this change. First identify the behavior to test, then write the failing test before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a Bug with RED First
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the tdd-workflow skill to reproduce this bug as a failing test. Do not change production code until the failure is clear.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor After Tests Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the tdd-workflow skill. Once tests pass, refactor duplication, naming, and structure without changing behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Multi-Agent TDD Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the tdd-workflow skill to split the task into RED, GREEN, and REFACTOR responsibilities. Review each phase before continuing.

output_examples.output: The assistant first describes a failing test for empty input, then implements the smallest validation change, then suggests naming cleanup.

output_examples.input: Fix a calculation bug in a billing module.
output_examples.output: The assistant writes a regression test that demonstrates the wrong total, makes the minimal fix, and confirms related edge cases.
The assistant keeps existing behavior covered, extracts duplication, improves names, and checks that tests remain green after each change.

best_practices: Ask the agent to state the expected behavior before writing tests.
Keep each TDD loop small enough to review in one step.
Run the relevant test suite after GREEN and after REFACTOR.
anti_patterns: Do not request production code before the failing test is defined.
Do not accept broad refactors while tests are failing.
Do not test private implementation details when behavior tests are possible.

faq.question: Does this skill run tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides the workflow. The agent must still use project commands to run tests.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: It is language neutral. It can guide TDD in any project with a test framework.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude Code, Claude, and Codex as supported tools.

faq.question: Is it useful for bug fixes?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends writing a failing regression test before changing production code.

faq.question: Does it include example test code?
faq.answer: No. It provides principles and workflow rules, not framework-specific examples.

faq.question: When should I avoid TDD?
faq.answer: Avoid strict TDD for quick exploration, UI layout experiments, or unclear requirements that need a spike first.
